flashcards.Back: First Hop Redundancy Protocol. A class of protocols that allow multiple physical routers to appear as a single virtual gateway for a group of hosts.
Hot Standby Router Protocol. A Cisco-proprietary FHRP that provides default gateway redundancy in an active/standby configuration.
Virtual Router Redundancy Protocol. An industry-standard (IETF) alternative to Cisco's HSRP, also using an active/standby model.
Gateway Load Balancing Protocol. A Cisco-proprietary FHRP that allows for active load balancing across multiple routers in a redundancy group.
A single IP address shared among multiple FHRP-enabled routers. Hosts configure this address as their default gateway.
A specialized MAC address associated with the FHRP group. The active router responds to ARP requests for the VIP with this MAC.
The specific router in an HSRP group that is currently responsible for forwarding all traffic sent to the virtual gateway address.
The router that monitors the active router and is prepared to take over the forwarding role immediately if the active router fails.
A value (0-255, default 100) used to elect the active router. The device with the highest priority wins the election.
An optional configuration that allows a higher-priority router to reclaim the active role as soon as it comes back online after a failure.
The destination address used for HSRPv1 hello packets, which is 224.0.0.2.
The destination address used for HSRPv2 hello packets, which is 224.0.0.102. it supports more groups than v1.
A packet sent by a router when it becomes the active forwarder to inform local switches to update their MAC tables for the virtual MAC.
The interval at which HSRP routers send health-check messages. The default is 3 seconds.
The time a standby router waits without hearing a Hello before assuming the active router has failed. The default is 10 seconds.
An identifier used to distinguish between different HSRP instances on the same subnet. it is also used to derive the virtual MAC address.
The primary router in a GLBP group responsible for answering ARP requests and assigning different virtual MACs to different hosts.
A router in a GLBP group that is responsible for forwarding traffic sent to its assigned virtual MAC address.
A feature that allows an FHRP router to monitor a specific interface or route. if the tracked object fails, the router lowers its priority to trigger a failover.
The standard format for HSRP v1 MAC addresses: 0000.0C07.ACxx (where xx is the hex group number).
The standard format for HSRP v2 MAC addresses: 0000.0C9F.Fxxx (where xxx is the hex group number).
An intermediate HSRP state where a router is not the active or standby device but is listening for Hello packets from other group members.
An HSRP state where a router periodically sends Hello packets and is actively participating in the election of the active and standby routers.
The starting state of HSRP when the protocol is first enabled on an interface or after a configuration change.
The VRRP term for the router that is actively forwarding packets for the virtual gateway address (equivalent to HSRP Active).
The VRRP term for routers that are monitoring the Master and ready to take over if it fails.
The automatic process where a standby router assumes the active role after the primary router stops sending hello packets.
The amount by which a router's priority is reduced when a tracked object fails, typically configured to be large enough to trigger an election.
A manual load-balancing technique where two HSRP groups are configured on the same subnet, with each router being active for one group and standby for the other.
A message a router might send to a host to suggest a better next hop. FHRPs typically disable these to ensure traffic stays within the redundant group.
